danger_signals.visual_form: Ghosts in Haunted Mansion stage; Pirates in Pirates of the Caribbean stage; Enemy ships in Space Mountain stage
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat requiring combat or evasion

danger_signals.visual_form: Asteroids in Space Mountain; Track obstacles in Big Thunder Mountain; Road obstacles in Autopia
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental danger requiring player skill to navigate safely

danger_signals.visual_form: Panhandle Pete in Autopia race stage; Ghosts and pirates as stage bosses
danger_signals.meaning: Major threat requiring defeat or avoidance to progress

danger_signals.visual_form: Pirates throwing cannonballs; Ghost attacks; Enemy ship fire

safety_signals.visual_form: Silver keys obtained upon stage completion
safety_signals.meaning: Progress toward goal; visible measure of advancement

safety_signals.visual_form: Children positioned around park offering trivia questions
safety_signals.meaning: Safe interaction zones; knowledge-based progression opportunities

safety_signals.visual_form: Disney character sprites in overworld hub
safety_signals.meaning: Quest authority figures; narrative safety and legitimacy

safety_signals.visual_form: Central locked gate structure in overworld
safety_signals.meaning: Final objective location; visual anchor for quest completion

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Mickey Mouse character sprite; Cinderella Castle gate
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Disneyland-style theme park setting; parade preparation atmosphere

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Pirate character sprites with distinctive attack animations
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple pirate types with different attack patterns (cannonball throwing, barrel dropping)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Pirates of the Caribbean themed island stage; tropical/shipwreck aesthetics

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Haunted dishes and silverware; evil-looking faces; cats in graveyard
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Haunted Mansion stage; gothic architecture; graveyard elements

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple enemy ship types in Space Mountain stage
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: First-person spaceship interior; asteroid field; space setting

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Cinderella Castle gate locked; six silver keys shown as unobtained; park in pre-parade state
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mickey requests help; children offer trivia; stages remain accessible for completion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Silver key obtained as stage reward; inventory reflects collected keys; progressive gate unlock anticipated
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Players defeat stage enemies and bosses; navigate obstacles; answer trivia for final key

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Cinderella Castle gate opens; Disney parade begins as finale
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mickey congratulates player; parade animation triggers; game concludes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Top-down perspective of Disneyland-style park; visible stage entrances; NPCs stationed throughout
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Free navigation between stages; NPC interaction available; stage selection at player discretion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Perspective shifts based on stage type: side-scrolling (Haunted Mansion, Pirates), top-down vehicle (Autopia, Big Thunder Mountain), first-person (Space Mountain)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Combat, platforming, racing, or navigation mechanics activate; enemies actively threaten; stage-specific hazards manifest

environmental_storytelling.example: Each Disney ride attraction (Haunted Mansion, Pirates of the Caribbean, Space Mountain, Autopia, Big Thunder Mountain) is rendered as a distinct gameplay environment with matching visual aesthetics and enemy types that reinforce attraction theme
Silver keys as literal quest objects; Cinderella Castle gate as visual embodiment of final objective; six keys needed creates clear progression narrative
Mickey and Goofy serve as quest-givers and authority figures; Panhandle Pete and pirates are named antagonists; NPCs positioned to offer knowledge rewards, establishing social hierarchy
Pirates visually distinct from ghosts; cosmic enemies separate from terrestrial threats; environmental color and sprite design communicates which faction inhabits each stage

environmental_storytelling.technique: Unused asset hints at abandoned mechanics
environmental_storytelling.example: Unused 'ITEM' and 'PLESENT' graphics suggest original design included item rewards beyond keys; seventh key sprite indicates expanded key collection system was planned but cut

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Turns head side to side in watchful animation; maintains position
Holds cannonball overhead; throws when player approaches
Pops out of window; holds barrel overhead; throws downward
Two-frame walking animation; two actions only—standing still or charging forward (aggressive)
Can be defeated by candle projectiles; requires player offensive action
Fires phaser fire at player; requires dodging and counter-attack
Remain stationary; ask Disney trivia questions; reveal key location if answered correctly
Requests help; offers quest framing; congratulates upon completion

notes: This game uses a hub-and-spoke structure with thematic stage differentiation to create distinct gameplay and visual environments. The Disneyland/Magic Kingdom setting establishes a unified world-state frame. Danger is communicated through enemy sprite design and behavioral animations (head-turning lookout, throwing motions, charging attacks). Safety is indicated through NPC presence, key acquisition visuals, and the stable castle structure. Faction identity relies on visual theming (pirates in Caribbean stage, ghosts in mansion, aliens in space) rather than explicit color-coding or sigil systems. The game's unused assets (seventh key, item/present graphics) suggest an abandoned mechanic that would have created additional world-state complexity through alternative reward paths.
